disease_associations.disease: Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

druggability_notes: Primary druggability axis: MDM2/MDM4–p53 interaction. MDM2 inhibitors (MDM2-p53 antagonists, e.g., brigimadlin; Brightline-1 trial) reactivate TP53 in MDM2-amplified tumors such as dedifferentiated liposarcoma; MDM2 amplification serves as patient selection biomarker. ASPP family interactions (PPP1R13B/ASPP1 and TP53BP2/ASPP2 activating; PPP1R13L/iASPP inhibitory) represent additional modulation points. Tetramerization domain noted as structural vulnerability in loss-of-function cancer contexts. Zinc/metal-binding and DNA-binding domains structurally characterized (3D structure available). Network pharmacology studies (quercetin, luteolin) report moderate predicted binding affinities to p53-related targets in heart failure context.
cellular_states: Cells undergoing DNA damage response (DSBs, UV, ionizing/gamma radiation)
Senescent cells (oncogene-induced, oxidative stress-induced, telomere stress-induced)
Cells in G1 and G2/M cell cycle arrest

Role in learning, memory, and pain signaling (hypersensitivity)

disease_associations.disease: Non-small cell lung cancer / lung adenocarcinoma
Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

druggability_notes: EGFR is a well-validated, well-established druggable receptor tyrosine kinase with an ATP-binding nucleotide-binding pocket in the cytoplasmic kinase domain. Small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) targeting this domain are in clinical use (e.g., afatinib for EGFR-mutant NSCLC, including uncommon mutations such as G719A). The bispecific antibody amivantamab is approved for EGFR-mutant NSCLC. Quinazolinone derivatives with SAR-optimized substituents act as potent dual EGFR/VEGFR inhibitors and can overcome drug resistance. Natural-product inhibitors have also been evaluated via molecular docking against EGFR PDB 1XKK (e.g., aspidinol binding affinity −8.6 kcal/mol). Oncogenic constitutively active forms (EGFRvIII; ligand-responsive cancer variants; overexpressed EGFR) represent key druggable disease contexts. EGFR inhibition also suppresses microbiota-induced proliferative effects in pancreatic cancer.

disease_associations.disease: B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (FOXF1/FENDRR subtype, IGH::FENDRR rearrangement)
T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia / mixed phenotype acute leukemia (t(14;16)(q32;q24), BCL11B enhancer hijacking)
Alveolar capillary dysplasia with misalignment of pulmonary veins
Developmental disorder associated with FOXF1 variants

druggability_notes: FOXF1/2-mediated enhancer remodeling is proposed as a key target for modulating senescent cells to promote healthy aging; FOXF1 acts via epigenetic cofactor interactions (p300/CBP, encoded by EP300/CREBBP), which represent potential intervention points. In leukemia subtypes overexpressing FOXF1, subtype-specific targeted interventions and early immunotherapy (e.g., blinatumomab) are suggested. The FOXF1-EZH2-DKK3 axis and iron/ROS-FOXF1 feedback loop are proposed as therapeutic targets in renal and pulmonary fibrosis. As a nuclear DNA-binding transcription factor, it is generally considered a challenging direct therapeutic target with no direct small-molecule binding pocket described.

pathways: RAS signaling downstream of NF1 loss-of-function variants
druggability_notes: KRAS is targeted by covalent inhibitor ARS-1620 (KRAS G12C-class; profiled in H358 cells; ALDH1A3 identified as off-target via C314 modification). Structure-based virtual screening identified KRAS-targeting peptide Peptide-1, showing binding via hydrogen bonding/hydrophobic interactions and antiproliferative activity in CRC. KRASi acquired resistance is mediated by ATF3-ASNS asparagine metabolic reprogramming; PERK inhibition synergizes with KRASi. Druggable mutant subgroups include KRAS G12C and Q61 variants (Q61H, Q61L). Post-translational lipid modifications (prenylation, palmitoylation) are relevant for membrane targeting. KRAS is recognized by LZTR1 for ubiquitination via the BCR (BTB-CUL3-RBX1) E3 ligase complex, suggesting a targeted protein degradation angle. 3D structure is available.
cellular_states: Upper hair follicle stem cells (skin tumor cell of origin)

disease_associations.disease: Hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome
Fanconi anemia / Fanconi anemia complementation group S

druggability_notes: BRCA1-deficient (homologous recombination-deficient) tumors are sensitive to PARP inhibitors (e.g., olaparib, niraparib, fluzoparib) via synthetic lethality. Key druggable structural/functional domains include the RING domain (zinc-finger, zinc ion binding) and coiled-coil region (PALB2 interaction). PARP inhibitor resistance arises from secondary intragenic mutations restoring partial BRCA1 protein function. In BRCA1/2-WT ccRCC, SLFN11 upregulation boosts proteasomal degradation of BRCA1, creating acquired PARP-1 dependency that sensitizes tumors to alkylating agent + PARP inhibitor combination (e.g., TMZ + olaparib). BRCA1 is an E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase (transferase class); loss-of-function mutations underlie synthetic lethality with PARP inhibitors in the clinical setting.
cellular_states: Early embryonic cells during maternal-to-zygotic transition
